name: Quarry Opposite Smith's Ferry, Tracksite
short_description: Early Jurassic tracksite and abandoned quarry near the Connecticut River with exposed sedimentary layers yielding quartz, feldspar, and mica. A unique location for rockhounds interested in both geo...
description:
This quarry site opposite Smith's Ferry is located in the Connecticut River Valley, one of Massachusetts' most geologically significant regions. The exposed quarry faces reveal sedimentary deposits from the Early Jurassic period, approximately 200 million years ago, when this area was part of a vast rift basin. The tracksite is known for fossilized dinosaur and amphibian footprints preserved in the ancient mudstones and sandstones. Rockhounders visiting this location can collect quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens from the weathered faces and talus slopes.

The Connecticut River Valley rift basin was formed during the breakup of the supercontinent Pangaea. As the continental plates pulled apart, sedimentary material was deposited in a series of deep lakes and shallow water environments. Mineral-bearing fluids percolated through these sediments, creating quartz veins and feldspar-rich layers. The gentle slope and accessible quarry walls make this an excellent site for collectors of all levels.

Best collection occurs along the base of the quarry walls where weathering has loosened specimens, and in the small streams and drainage areas that cut through the property. The mica flakes are particularly abundant and often display excellent cleavage. Quartz crystals can range from small points to larger milky specimens. The historical significance of the tracksite adds educational value to any rockhounding visit.

Access is straightforward from South Hadley. The site is relatively flat with gentle slopes, making it accessible to most visitors. Be respectful of the geological and paleontological importance of this location and avoid disturbing in-situ fossils or major stratigraphic features.
history: This quarry was actively worked in the 19th and early 20th centuries for building stone and aggregate material. The Connecticut River Valley rift basin formed during Pangaean breakup in the Early Jurassic, creating ideal conditions for preserving both sedimentary minerals and trace fossils from ancient reptiles and amphibians that inhabited these early lakes.
tips: Search the talus slopes and loose material at the base of quarry walls first, as weathering naturally exposes the best specimens. Bring a small chisel or pry bar to carefully extract quartz and feldspar specimens from softer sedimentary matrix, but avoid hammering large blocks. Look for the mica flakes in the finer-grained, darker layers - they often peel away naturally with light pressure. The drainage channel running through the property concentrates heavier minerals; scan this area for partially rounded quartz pebbles and feldspar fragments.
terrain_notes: Elevation 118 ft with gentle slopes throughout the site. Terrain consists of exposed quarry walls, talus slopes, and loose sedimentary debris. Walking surface is uneven with scattered rocks and loose stones. Small drainage channels and seasonal water flow present. Footing is stable on relatively flat areas but requires care on steeper slopes. Vegetation is moderate scrub and small trees. Site is approximately 0.2 miles from parking area.
best_season: April - October. Spring through fall offers dry conditions and stable footing. Summer can be hot and humid. Late fall brings rain that may create slippery footing on sedimentary slopes. Winter freezing and thaw cycles are minimal in this lower elevation location but can make access muddy from November through March.
written_directions: From South Hadley, take Route 47 south toward the Connecticut River. Continue approximately 1.5 miles until you approach the area near Smith's Ferry. Look for the abandoned quarry on the west side of the road marked by exposed pale sedimentary cliffs and talus slopes. Parking is available at a small pullout near the quarry entrance. GPS coordinates: 42.261906, -72.602875.
road_conditions: Paved highway (Route 47) to pullout. Small gravel or dirt parking area. No high-clearance vehicle required.
parking_notes: Small gravel pullout accommodating 3-4 vehicles near quarry entrance on Route 47. No formal parking lot or facilities.
collection_rules: This is a public site with no formal restrictions documented. Collect personal-use specimens only. Respect the geological and paleontological significance of the tracksite. Do not hammer or damage major outcrops or stratigraphy. Avoid collecting in-situ trace fossils or disturbing bone beds. All collection is informal and voluntary compliance-based.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Do not damage major rock faces or outcrops
Respect paleontological features and trace fossils
No commercial collection
Leave the site as you found it
nearest_services: South Hadley town center approximately 2 miles north. Gas, food, and water available in town.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers. Uneven rocky footing requires sturdy shoes but no technical climbing. Small elevation changes and loose rocks present minor trip hazard. Accessible to families and those with basic mobility. Parking is very close to collecting areas.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can I find at the quarry opposite Smith's Ferry in Massachusetts?
faq.answer: This Early Jurassic tracksite yields quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. Quartz crystals range from small points to larger milky specimens, feldspar fragments are common in the lighter-colored sedimentary layers, and mica flakes with excellent cleavage are particularly abundant in the fine-grained darker layers.

faq.question: Is the Smith's Ferry tracksite near South Hadley free to collect at?
faq.answer: Yes, this is a public rockhounding site with no collection fees or permits required. Personal-use collection is allowed at no cost, making it an affordable option for casual rockhounders and families.

faq.question: How do I find quartz and feldspar at this Massachusetts quarry?
faq.answer: Search the talus slopes and loose material at the base of quarry walls first. Use a small chisel to carefully extract specimens from the softer sedimentary matrix. The drainage channel running through the property concentrates heavier minerals and is an excellent place to find partially rounded quartz pebbles.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at the Smith's Ferry tracksite?
faq.answer: April through October offers the best conditions with dry footing and stable terrain. Spring and summer are ideal, though summer can be humid. Avoid winter months when freezing and thaw cycles plus rain create muddy, slippery footing.

faq.question: Is the South Hadley Smith's Ferry quarry suitable for beginners and children?
faq.answer: Yes, this is an excellent beginner and family-friendly location. The gentle slopes, easy access, and abundant loose specimens make it perfect for children ages 6 and up. No special skills or experience required to collect quality minerals.

faq.question: What tools and safety equipment do I need for rockhounding at this Connecticut River Valley site?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots for uneven rocky footing, a small chisel or pry bar for extracting specimens, a geology hammer, and a collecting bag. The site has loose rocks and sharp material, so wear long pants and gloves. No special permits or equipment licenses are needed for personal collection.

meta_title: Smith's Ferry Quarry Rockhounding - South Hadley MA
meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at the public Smith's Ferry tracksite quarry in South Hadley, Massachusetts. Free collecting on Jurassic sedimentary formations near the Connecticut River.
